coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic fuel cell with cathodic P-BiVO4/CQDs and anodic WO3 for efficient Cr(VI) reduction and stable electricity generation
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Xin
Du, Hao
Tan, Meng
Qian, Jianying
Deng, Man
Hao, Derek
Wang, Qi
Zhu, Huayue
coredata.dc:description: Developing efficient and sustainable photoelectrodes in photocatalytic fuel cell (PFC) system remains a major challenge. In this work, a PFC system (WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs) was established based on WO3 photoanode and P-BiVO4 photocathode loaded with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for Cr(VI) reduction with simultaneous electricity production. The photocatalytic performance of the constructed PFC system was characterized by various analytical methods. With the introduction of CQDs, both Cr(VI) reduction and maximum power density (P max) have been significantly enhanced. When the loading amount of CQDs was 150 μL, its Cr(VI) reduction rate and maximum power density (P max) were respectively 90 % and 0.10 mW/cm2 within 180 min of illumination, superior to the WO3|P-BiVO4 system. This was attributed to the decoration of CQDs, which broadened the light absorption range, increased the isolation efficiency of charge carriers and ultimately enhanced the ability of the fabricated PFC system. The superior stability and practicability were also demonstrated by five cyclic experiments. In addition, the plausible mechanism was proposed. This work offers an important reference for the application of the PFC system in wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic fuel cell
P-BiVO4/CQDs photocathode
WO3 photoanode
Cr(Ⅵ) reduction
Electricity production

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully thank the finical support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21876154 and 22276168), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang (JRK22001). The authors would like to thank the Shiyanjia lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM tests.
We gratefully thank the finical support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21876154 ). The authors would like to thank the Shiyanjia lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the TEM tests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of P-BiVO4/CQDs, (b and c) SEM and EDS mapping images of P-BiVO4/CQDs, (d) TEM image of CQDs, (e) XRD patterns of WO3 and P-BiVO4/CQDs and (f) FT-IR spectra of P-BiVO4 and P-BiVO4/CQDs.
High-resolution XPS spectra of P-BiVO4 and P-BiVO4/CQDs: (a) C 1 s, (b) O 1 s, (c) Bi 4f and (d) V 2p.
(a) UV–vis DRS spectra of WO3 and P-BiVO4, (b) Tauc plots of P-BiVO4 and WO3, (c) the band architecture of WO3 and P-BiVO4 and (d) PL spectra of P-BiVO4 and P-BiVO4/CQDs.

The transient i-t curve (a) and the EIS (b) of P-BiVO4/CQDs photocathode, the Voc (c and d) and Jsc (e) curves of WO3|Pt, Pt| P-BiVO4/CQDs, and WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs system and (f) LSV and polarization curves of WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs system.
(a) Cr(VI) reduction kinetics by different electrode systems; the effects of (b) CQDs loading amounts, (c) pH value and (e) initial Cr(VI) concentrations; corresponding rate constants (d and f).
(a) Recycling tests of WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs and (b) XRD patterns of fresh and used samples.

M−S plots of (a) WO3 and (b) P-BiVO4, (c) the effect of trapping agent on Cr(VI) reduction, (d) ESR spectra by DMPO-
O2 −, (e) Temporal UV–Vis absorbance spectra and (f) Fluorescence spectra by WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs system.
The proposed charge transfer mechanism of WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs.
Schematic representation for the construction of P-BiVO4/CQDs composites.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic fuel cell with cathodic P-BiVO4/CQDs and anodic WO3 for efficient Cr(VI) reduction and stable electricity generation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Zhejiang Gongshang University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, RMIT University, Melbourne, VIC 3000, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, RMIT University, Melbourne, VIC 3000, Australia

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing efficient and sustainable photoelectrodes in photocatalytic fuel cell (PFC) system remains a major challenge. In this work, a PFC system (WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs) was established based on WO3 photoanode and P-BiVO4 photocathode loaded with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for Cr(VI) reduction with simultaneous electricity production. The photocatalytic performance of the constructed PFC system was characterized by various analytical methods. With the introduction of CQDs, both Cr(VI) reduction and maximum power density (P max) have been significantly enhanced. When the loading amount of CQDs was 150 μL, its Cr(VI) reduction rate and maximum power density (P max) were respectively 90 % and 0.10 mW/cm2 within 180 min of illumination, superior to the WO3|P-BiVO4 system. This was attributed to the decoration of CQDs, which broadened the light absorption range, increased the isolation efficiency of charge carriers and ultimately enhanced the ability of the fabricated PFC system. The superior stability and practicability were also demonstrated by five cyclic experiments. In addition, the plausible mechanism was proposed. This work offers an important reference for the application of the PFC system in wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium (Cr(VI)) is extensively applied in electroplating, metal processing and other industries [1]. Cr(VI)-containing pollutants are readily released into natural water body because of its good solubility and high toxicity, thus threatening human health and ecosystem [2]. For the Cr(VI)-containing wastewaters, the common practice is to reduce toxic Cr(VI) to Cr(III), and the toxicity can be reduced by more than 100 times [3], which has received extensive attention. Due to its feasible operation, cost-effectiveness, high efficiency, and favorable sustainability, photocatalytic technology is promising for environmental purification [4–7]. In general, Cr(VI) can capture photogenerated electrons forming low toxic Cr(III) by photocatalysis, following by Cr(III) remediation through precipitation process [8,9]. However, the chemical energy generated in the process of wastewater treatment cannot be directly used. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a method that can not only remove pollutants but also recover energy from wastewater [10,11].

Photocatalytic fuel cell (PFC) is a newly developed sewage treatment device based on photocatalytic technology that generates electricity simultaneously under sunlight irradiation [12–14]. In such a system, electrons generated by the photoanode can be transferred to the cathode through an external circuit to generate electricity [15,16]. At the same time, the holes or hydroxyl radicals (
OH) produced by the reaction between H2O and holes took part in the degradation of organic compounds [17]. As the core component of PFC, the photoelectrodes were responsible for PFC performance. Hence, constructing high-efficiency photoelectrodes was extremely significant for PFC system [18,19]. Among numerous materials, TiO2 photoanode and Pt cathode are the most commonly used materials in PFC [13,20,21], but the challenge remains due to the narrow spectral response range of TiO2 and high cost of Pt [22,23]. P-type semiconductors such as Cu2O and CuBi2O4 were often applied as photocathodes, while their applications were often limited by the difficulty of component control, cumbersome synthesis and poor stability [24]. As a copper-free semiconductor, p-type BiVO4 (P-BiVO4) possesses facile synthesis, high reduction ability and good stability, making it a potential alternative to copper-based photocathodes [25].

Unfortunately, the photocatalytic activity of the PFC system was always hampered by the weak conductivity and rapid charge recombination of a single component. For this purpose, many strategies have been adopted, such as element doping, defect engineering, and the decoration of co-catalysts to achieve highly active PFC system [26]. Among which, loading cocatalysts is one of the most promising approaches to boost its photocatalytic performance [27]. As a new kind of carbon nano-material, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have stable chemical stability, low cost and low inherent toxicity, and it can also significantly broaden light absorption range and accelerate interfacial charge transfer [28–30]. For example, Hu et al. [31] designed a dual-photoelectrode photoelectrocatalytic system based on TiO2 nanoneedle arrays photoanode and nitrogen-doped carbon dots/Co3O4 photocathode for efficient sulfadiazine (SDZ) degradation. For the potential difference of quasi-Femi energy between two electrodes, the catalytic performance could be improved. Li et al. [32] successfully fabricated a novel Vis-NIR light-responsive CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets, under visible light illumination, the photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) efficiency reached 83 % over the optimal composite, which was nearly seven times higher than that of pristine Sb2WO6. CQDs extensively promoted the separation of photo generated charge carriers. Given the excellent properties of CQDs, p-type BiVO4 modified with CQDs was a favorable photocathode for PFC systems, when the electrons are excited from the valence band of P-BiVO4 to the conduction band, they can be collected by CQDs, thus extending the lifetime of electrons [32], effectively improve the electron transfer in the photocatalytic system, thereby improving the reduction ability of Cr(VI).

As a typical n-type semiconductor, WO3 has a bandgap energy of 2.5–2.7 eV. which can absorb light up to 500 nm in the visible spectrum [33,34], and it has good photo absorption ability and high electronic migration rate. Even under light illumination, it also shows high light corrosion resistance and good chemical stability in acidic aqueous solution (pH < 3) [35], these characteristics make it to be a very promising photoanode material for PFC system.

In this work, we selected WO3 as photoanode and CQDs decorated P-BiVO4 as photocathode to fabricate PFC system (WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs) for Cr(VI) reduction and simultaneous electricity production. For a Fermi energy level difference between two electrodes, this system can effectively reduce the recombination of electrons and holes. And the introduction of CQDs not only can broaden the light response range but also boost the isolation rate of charge carriers. The effects of the content of CQDs, pollutant concentrations and initial solution pH were also delved under visible light illumination. Finally, a plausible charge transfer mechanism was proposed, this work potentially provides an efficient way to establish a PFC hybrid system for wastewater treatment without any external bias.
All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification, and can be found in SI (Text. S1).
WO3 photoanode was prepared according to the previous literature (Fig. S1) [36]. Detailed procedure can be seen in Text S2.

Synthesis of P-BiVO4 photocathode: P-BiVO4 photocathode was prepared according to the previous report [25]. Typically, 0.291 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 0.2232 g EDTA-2Na were together dissolved in 12 mL nitric acid to get a solution A. At the same time, 0.0702 g NH4VO3 and 0.2232 g EDTA-2Na were dissolved in 24 mL of NaOH solution to get a solution B. The solution A was added into solution B to get a mixed solution. After that, the resulting mixture and FTO glasses were transported to a Teflon-lined autoclave, followed by heating at 180 °C for 18 h. Finally, the obtained sample was cleaned with deionized water and ethanol alternatively, and dried to get the P-BiVO4 film.

Synthesis of CQDs: 9 g glucose was added in 50 mL deionized water, followed by adding 1 M NaOH (50 mL) to form a mixture. The mixed solution was sonicated until the solution became brown–red, and its pH was adjusted to 7 with HCl, followed by adding ethanol (100 mL). A semi-diaphragm (MWCO 1000) was utilized for dialysis treatment until the obtained solution turned brown, indicating CQDs was formed.
Different contents of CQDs were uniformly drop-coated on the P-BiVO4 film and dried at 40 °C for 5 h to obtain the P-BiVO4/CQDs photocathode (Scheme 1 ).

X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Smart Lab SE) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) were applied to investigate the crystal phases and surface chemical properties, respectively. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS, Hitachi U-3900) were measured to indicate the optical properties. Fourier transformation infrared spectra (FT-IR) were used to characterize their chemical functional. Scan electronic microscopes (SEM, sigma 300) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20) were applied to observe their surface morphologies and microstructures.

The performance of the PFC system was tested by Cr(VI) reduction (Fig. 8). The as-prepared WO3 photoanode and P-BiVO4/CQDs photocathode were introduced to a 50 mL Cr(VI) solution (40 μmol/L). Herein, Na2SO4 (0.1 M) and EDTA-2Na (2 mM) were respectively applied as the electrolyte and hole scavenger. The solution pH was adjusted to 3 by 1 M H2SO4 or NaOH. Before illumination, the solution was stirred for 30 min in darkness to achieve the equilibrium between adsorption and desorption. After that, the solution was illuminated for 180 min. Each 30 min, the solution sample was taken out at a set time and determined by the 1,5-diphenylcarbanyl dihydrazide colorimetric method at λmax = 545 nm [37–39] using UV2012PC spectrophotometer. The electron spin resonance (ESR) was used to capture
O2 − in methanol suspension of photoelectrode by 5,5′-dimethyl-1-pirroline-N-oxide (DMPO) [40].

The as-prepared P-BiVO4/CQDs and P-BiVO4 were firstly characterized by SEM images (Fig. 1 a and Fig. S2) to investigate microstructure and surface morphology. P-BiVO4/CQDs and P-BiVO4 displayed a morphology of nanoparticles. The particles are highly faceted and tetragonal prism structure with a width of 3–4 μm and length of 4–6 μm, and the thickness of the P-BiVO4/CQDs film is about 0.5–1 μm. CQD clusters can be clearly seen on P-BiVO4/CQDs film surface. Moreover, EDX elemental mapping exhibited evenly distribution of Bi, O, V and C elements in the P-BiVO4/CQDs composite (Fig. 1b and 1c). It can be seen form TEM images (Fig. 1d) that CQDs were uniformly dispersed on P-BiVO4 surface and the clear lattice fringes spacing of 0.302 nm can be attributed to the (100) planes of CQDs [28], further evidencing the successful decoration of CQDs.

XRD patterns were performed on the prepared WO3 and P-BiVO4/CQDs to characterize their crystal structures. As shown in Fig. 1e, the diffraction peaks at 23.36°, 23.8°, 24.6°, 29.08°, 41.9°,51.68° were respectively assigned to the (001), (020), (200), (111), (221) and (140) facets of WO3, indicating the monoclinic phase of WO3 [36]. For P-BiVO4, the diffraction peaks at 19.1°, 30.8°, 33.8°, 37.9°, 40.1° and 47.4° were respectively attributed to the (101), (211), (112), (202), (301) and (302) planes of P-BiVO4 [24,25]. After the decoration of CQDs, P-BiVO4/CQDs exhibited similar diffraction peaks to P-BiVO4, but the peak of CQDs was not observed, probably due to the low amount of CQDs.

FT-IR measurements were performed to indicate the surface functional groups of differentsamples (Fig. 1f). For P-BiVO4 electrode, the typical peaks at 735 cm−1, 1400 cm−1 and 3116 cm−1 respectively corresponding to Bi-O, C = O and C–H were clearly seen, demonstrating its successful synthesis. The similar typical peaks to P-BiVO4 were detected in P-BiVO4/CQDs sample. But unlike P-BiVO4, the peak intensity was enhanced with the decoration of CQDs, furthering proving its successful loading. In addition, the weak peaks at 1040 cm−1 and 3460 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration between C–H and O–H, respectively.

XPS spectra were analyzed to investigate their element compositions and chemical states. As observed, C, O, Bi and V elements in the survey spectra were presented in P-BiVO4/CQDs composition (Fig. S3). For P-BiVO4/CQDs, the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2 a) can be divided into three peaks with binding energies of 284.8, 286.38 and 288.08 eV, respectively corresponding to the C–C, C-O and O-C = O bond [41]. In O 1 s spectra (Fig. 2b), the peaks at 530.08, 531.08 and 532.68 eV were ascribed to the Bi-O, C = O and C-O bond, respectively. For Bi 4f (Fig. 2c), the two signals at 158.58 eV and 163.88 eV were ascribed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of Bi3+ [42]. For V 2p spectrum (Fig. 2d), the signals at 515.48, 516.98 and 518.58 eV were related to V 2p3/2 of V5+, and the signal at 522.38 eV was related to V 2p1/2 of V4+, indicating partial self-reduction of V5+ [25]. In contrast to P-BiVO4, the negative shifting of O 1 s, Bi 4f and V 2p in P-BiVO4/CQDs evidenced the strong interaction between P-BiVO4 and CQDs.

UV–Vis DRS was utilized to study their optical absorption properties, and the results were shown in Fig. 3 a and 3b. Both WO3 and P-BiVO4 had a good light absorption capacity, which was consistent with their color. Moreover, the bandgaps (E g) of WO3 and P-BiVO4 were respectively estimated to be 2.76 and 2.85 eV according to a Tauc plot equation (Fig. 3c). Also, according to Fig.S4, the bandgap energy for the P-BiVO4/CQDs is 2.29 eV. From Fig.S5, the CB and VB positions of P-BiVO4/CQDs are respectively −0.08 V vs. Ag/AgCl and 2.21 V vs. Ag/AgCl.

The PL spectra of the P-BiVO4 and P-BiVO4/CQDs electrodes (Fig. 3d) were measured at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm, and it can be seen that the peak of the P-BiVO4/CQDs electrode is significantly lower than the P-BiVO4 electrode, which indicating that P-BiVO4/CQDs electrodes had higher charge separation efficiency with the introduction of CQDs.

In order to explore the effect of the loading amount of CQDs on the photoelectrochemical properties of P-BiVO4/CQDs electrodes, transient photocurrent responses (Fig. 4 a) and EIS (Fig. 4b) were characterized. As observed, the photocurrent responses were enhanced with increasing the loading amount of CQDs from 0 to 150 μL, which was increased by nearly 1.6 times compared with pure P-BiVO4. While when loading amount was 200 μL, the photocurrent density decreased. Excessive CQDs may block incident light, thereby inhibiting the light absorption of the P-BiVO4 layer [43]. Moreover, to characterize the interfacial charge transfer behavior of the as-prepared samples, EIS tests were also performed. In general, the smaller semicircle in Nyquist plots, the smaller the charge transfer resistance [44,45]. As seen, the P-BiVO4/CQDs electrode with 150 μL loading amount displayed the smallest semicircle, further confirming superior interfacial charge transfer and excellent photocatalytic activity. The above results were also in good agreement with those of transient photocurrent density.

The Voc and Jsc of the WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs system were measured in a 0.1 mol/L Na2SO4 solution (pH = 7.0). As shown in Fig. 4c, the Voc of the photocathode of WO3 and P-BiVO4/CQDs were −0.009 V and 0.001 V respectively in darkness, and increased to −0.01 V and 0.016 V under light illumination. While the Voc of WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs system under light illumination was 0.092 V (Fig. 4d). This result showed that the WO3 photoanode as an n-type semiconductor and P-BiVO4/CQDs as a p-type semiconductor provided negative and positive bias, respectively. In addition, according to energy band theory, the Fermi levels of the n-type and p-type semiconductor were close to its conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB), respectively. In order to obtain the Fermi level equilibrium, photoelectrons will migrate from n-type semiconductor and p-type semiconductor when they were connected by the conductive wire, thus forming a voltage due to the Fermi level difference between the two electrodes [46]. Therefore, this entire system can be performed only under light conditions without applying external potential.

For WO3|Pt and Pt|P-BiVO4/CQDs system (Fig. 4e), no obvious photocurrent response was observed in darkness. While the WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs system can respond quickly once it was illuminated, and the transient photocurrent response was 0.0005 mA/cm2, and tended to obtain a balance at 0.003 mA/cm2. Therefore, these obtained results proved that the self-driving operation of the WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs system was the result of the synergistic interaction of WO3 photoanode and P-BiVO4/CQDs photocathode. And it can be seen in Fig. 4f, the WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs system produced the highest power density (P max) of 0.10 mW/cm2, superior to the WO3|P-BiVO4 (Fig. S6). Interestingly, both current density and P max remarkably decreased with the introduction of oxygen (Fig. S7). Fill factor (FF), an important parameter for evaluating the quality of PFC, reflects the variation characteristics of the output power of the battery. The closer the filling factor is to 1, the better the performance of the solar cell [18]. It can be calculated according to Eq. (1).
(1)
F F =
P
max
V
OC
×
J
S C
= 0.29
Namely, the e− from the photoanode could be trapped partially by oxygen, and not migrate to the photocathode across the external circuit, thus leading to the lower current density and P max under O2 atmosphere.

The photocatalytic performance of the PFC system was evaluated by Cr(VI) reduction. The Cr(VI) reduction was performed both under visible light illumination but without the existence of catalysts and under dark but with the presence of catalysts. From Fig. S8, only a small amount of Cr(VI) was reduced. As displayed in Fig. 5 a, in the presence of only WO3 and P-BiVO4/CQDs photoelectrodes, the Cr(VI) removal efficiency were only 21 % and 29 % within 3 h of illumination, respectively. In the two-electrode system composed of WO3 and P-BiVO4, the Cr(VI) removal percentage was only 33 %, showing a slightly enhanced photocatalytic performance. When P-BiVO4 photocathode was modified by CQDs, the value can reach 90 %, which had the best photocatalytic performance, and also beyond the other reported PFC systems (Table S1). Nonetheless, when WO3 and P-BiVO4/CQDs were not connected, the removal rate of Cr(VI) was only 37 %, indirectly demonstrating that the Fermi energy level difference generated between WO3 and P-BiVO4/CQDs photoelectrode. And the Fermi energy level difference between these two photoelectrodes can form self-bias, thus allowing more electrons to participate in Cr(VI) reduction, which further curtailed the recombination of charge carriers [47].

In order to explore the effects of loading amounts of CQDs (from 0 μL to 200 μL), Cr(VI) reduction of was also performed on different P-BiVO4/CQDs electrodes, and the results were shown in Fig. 5b. WO3|P-BiVO4 system only removed 33 % Cr(VI) within 180 min. Cr(VI) reduction was enhanced with the introduction of CQDs. Interestingly, the reduction rate first increased and then declined with increasing the loading amount of CQDs. Excessive loading amount of CQDs might block the incident light absorption, thereby resulting in reduced photocatalytic performance [43]. A peak was achieved when the loading amount of CQDs was 150 μL. As shown in Fig. S9, the rate constant was estimated to be 0.01166 min−1 when loading amount of CQDs was 150 μL, which was ca. 5 times that of 0 μL CQDs, respectively. Coupled the results of photoelectrochemical measurements, we concluded that 150 μL was the optimal loading amount of CQDs.

The pH value may change the chemical states of Cr(VI) [48], so we investigated the Cr(VI) reduction dynamics under different pH values. The obtained results were shown in Fig. 5c-5d. The reduction ability of Cr(VI) in acidic environments was obviously better than that under alkaline conditions, meaning the acidic environment was beneficial for Cr(VI) reduction. Typically, under acidic conditions, Cr(VI) mainly existed as HCrO4 −, and the excessive H+ were consumed during the process of Cr(VI) reduction. While CrO4 2− was the main form under alkaline conditions, where the formed Cr(OH)3 precipitate might block the reactive sites of photocatalysts (Eqs. (2) and (3)), thus leading to the inferior Cr(VI) reduction performance. Besides, variable Cr(VI) concentrations were also characterized. We found that with the increase of initial Cr(VI) concentration (Fig. 5e-5f), the removal rate of Cr(VI) gradually decreased. In the case of fixed electrons, the lower of the Cr(VI) concentration, the more electrons were available [48]. The highest Cr(VI) reduction rate was obtained at 20 μmol/L.
(2)

The Cr(VI) reduction performance under different monochro-matic radiation irradiation was evaluated by using different filters, as illustrated in the Fig. S10. The WO3|P-BiVO4 system behaved a wide light response range and exhibited the best Cr(VI) removal efficiency at QD 420 nm. The oxidation ability of the system was also evaluated through the degradation of TC, as shown in the Fig. S11. Compared with P-BiVO4 system, P-BiVO4/CQDs exhibited enhanced oxidation ability and can degraded 60 % of TC within three hours, which was 5.5 times that of P-BiVO4 system.

The reusability and stability of P-BiVO4/CQDs photocathode were investigated and the results were displayed in Fig. 6 . The initial experimental conditions were fixed. Both WO3 photoanode and P-BiVO4/CQDs photocathode were washed and dried after each cycle, and the photocatalytic reduction experiments were repeated for 5 times (Fig. 6a). One can note that the photocatalytic ability of this photocathode has no considerable decrease after five cycles, more than 80 % Cr(VI) was still removed. The SEM image of P-BiVO4/CQDs after five cycles was shown in Fig. S12. As found, there is no significant change in the morphology before and after five cycles. Comparing the fresh and used P-BiVO4/CQDs electrodes, little difference can be observed in the characteristic XRD patterns (Fig. 6b). No significant change in XRD patterns were seen before and after the reaction, and all the diffraction peaks were associated to P-BiVO4 and CQDs, indicating the good chemical and structural stability of P-BiVO4/CQDs electrodes.

The M−S curves of WO3 and P-BiVO4 electrodes were displayed in Fig. 7
a–b. The positive slop suggested that WO3 was a n-type semiconductor, which could provide more electrons. On the contrary, P-BiVO4 was a p-type semiconductor as it has a negative slop. The E fb of WO3 and P-BiVO4 were estimated to be 0.51 V and 2.18 V, respectively. In generally, the CB position of a n-type semiconductor is 0.1 V more negative than the E fb. Whereas, as for p-type semiconductor, the VB position was 0.1 V more positive than the E fb [3,41]. Therefore, the CB of WO3 and VB of P-BiVO4 were 0.41 V and 2.28 V vs. Ag/AgCl, respectively, corresponding to 0.61 V and 2.48 V vs. NHE based on the following equation.
(4)
E
N H E
=
E
v s
A g / A g C l
+
0.197
V
+
0.0591
p H ⋯ ⋯ ⋯ ⋯ ⋯ ⋯ ⋯ ⋯

According to the results of UV–vis DRS, the calculated bandgaps (E g) of WO3 and P-BiVO4 were respectively 2.76 eV and 2.85 eV. Thus the VB of WO3 and CB of P-BiVO4 were calculated to be 3.37 V and −0.37 V vs. NHE, respectively. It follows from the above results that the difference in the Fermi energy level between the WO3 and the P-BiVO4 theoretically provides a sufficient driving force for Cr(VI) reduction.

To explore the role of active species during Cr(VI) reduction, radical quenching experiments were performed (Fig. 7a). In this constructed WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs system, AgNO3 and EDTA-2Na were used to trap electrons (e−) and holes (h+), respectively, N2 was bubbled into the system to remove dissolved oxygen, thereby indirectly demonstrating the role of
O− 2. The obtained results indicated that the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) remarkably decreased to 16 % when AgNO3 was added into the reaction system, while with the presence of EDTA-2Na, the photocatalytic rate can be 90 %, indicating that e− was responsible for Cr(VI) reduction and h+ was not the reason for Cr(VI) reduction. Oxygen can react with the photoelectrons to form
O− 2. After the introduction of N2, the removal efficiency was significantly impeded, implying that
O− 2 could accelerate Cr(VI) reduction. 69 % Cr(VI) was removed with the addition of EDTA-2Na, indicating that e− played a more critical role in Cr(VI) reduction than
O− 2.

In order to confirm the existence of
OH and
O− 2 involved in the Cr(VI) reduction process, ESR measurements were performed (Fig. 7d). As seen, a strong four peaks of DMPO-
O− 2 with of 1:2:2:1 ratio was observed under light condition, proving that the WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs system could generate
O− 2. In addition, the in-situ formation of
OH and
O− 2 in the system was determined with terephthalic acid (PL-TA) [49] and nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (UV-NBT) as molecular probes, respectively. One can note that the peak intensity of TA-
OH (Fig. 7e) increased with prolonging reaction time, while that of NBT-
O− 2 decreased (Fig. 7f). Overall, the above findings confirmed the formation of
OH and
O− 2 in this WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs system.

Based on the aforementioned analysis and discussion, a plausible charge transfer mechanism was proposed (Fig. 8). Under illumination, both WO3 photoanode and P-BiVO4/CQDs photocathode were excited to generate e− and h+, and the e− flowed in the CB of WO3 photoanode to the P-BiVO4/CQDs photocathode through the external circuit. According to the energy band structure, there was a Fermi energy level difference between WO3 and P-BiVO4/CQDs photoelectrode, the e− from the CB of WO3 and the h+ in the VB of P-BiVO4/CQDs electrode will easily recombine, while the h+ remaining in the VB of WO3 electrode can oxidize H2O to
OH since the VB position of the WO3 was positive enough than E H2O/
OH (+2.68 V vs. NHE). CQDs, an effective electronic inventory, can capture electrons emitted from the CB of P-BiVO4, thus inhibiting the e−/h+ pair recombination [50,51]. Hence, the migration of photo-excited e− between WO3, P-BiVO4, CQDs and the exterior loop enhanced the separation of charge carriers, thus promoting Cr(VI) reduction and electricity production. The remaining e− in the CB of P-BiVO4 can be transferred to O2 forming
O− 2 due to more negative CB position than E O2/
O− 2 (−0.33 V vs. NHE). In a word,
O− 2 and e− were the main species for Cr(VI) reduction based on the above discussion (Eqs. 5–8).
(5)
W O
3
/
P - B i V O
4
/ C Q D s +
h ν
→
e
-
+
h
+
(6)
H 2
O
+
h
+
→
· O H
+
H
+
(7)
O 2
+
e
-
→
· O
2
-
(8)
C r
( V I )
+
· O
2
-
/
e
-
→
C r
( I I I )

To sum up, the P-BiVO4 photocathode decorated with CQDs was successfully prepared and combined with WO3 photoanode to fabricate the visible light response PFC system (WO3|P-BiVO4/CQDs) for Cr(VI) reduction and simultaneous electricity production. Under the optimized experimental conditions, its Cr(VI) reduction efficiency and P max were respectively 90 % and 0.10 mW/cm2 within 180 min of visible-light irradiation, superior to the WO3|P-BiVO4 system. The introduction of CQDs can broaden the light response range as well as boost the isolation rate of charge carriers. In addition, five cycle experiments proved that the as-established PFC system has superior stability and potential practicability with negligible loss of activity. Finally, a plausible mechanism of charge transfer and reaction was proposed. Overall, this work provides a reference for constructing the highly efficient PFC system to reduce Cr(VI) and simultaneously produce electricity.

Xin Ma: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Methodology. Hao Du: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Meng Tan: Data curation. Jianying Qian: Visualization, Investigation. Man Deng: Data curation. Derek Hao: Writing – review & editing. Qi Wang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration. Huayue Zhu: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully thank the finical support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21876154 and 22276168), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang (JRK22001). The authors would like to thank the Shiyanjia lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM tests.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2024.126644.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.-M. Lam, J.-C. Sin, M.W. Warren Tong, H. Zeng, H. Li, L. Huang, H. Lin, J.-W. Lim, 2023. ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Yang, Y. Fu, F. Sun, M. Deng, C. Zhang, N. Li, D. Hao, Q. Wang, G. Zhuang, 2023. Preparation of novel diperylene-cored polyimide photocatalyst with broad-spectra response and high stability, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 639, 472-483.
